eli3: Regression testing for LLMs checks that your AI application doesn't get worse when you change something — a new model, a new prompt, a new retrieval strategy. Just like unit tests prevent software bugs from sneaking in, regression tests prevent quality bugs from sneaking into your AI system.

The challenge is that LLM outputs are not deterministic, so you can't compare outputs with exact equality. Instead, you compare quality metrics: did the average faithfulness score drop? Did the task success rate fall? These statistical comparisons tell you whether a change caused a regression.
LLM regression tests are best run in CI/CD pipelines: every time you change a prompt or upgrade a model, the tests run automatically and flag any quality regressions before they reach production.

whenWhy.title: Preventing quality regressions on prompt changes
whenWhy.body: Prompt engineering is iterative: you improve a prompt for one case and inadvertently break another. Without regression tests, this is invisible until users complain. With regression tests: maintain a golden dataset of (input, expected_output) pairs; run the evaluation suite on every prompt change; if any metric drops below baseline by more than a threshold, the CI check fails. This catches prompt regressions in minutes, not days.

whenWhy.title: Validating model upgrades before rollout
whenWhy.body: LLM providers regularly release new model versions (GPT-4o → GPT-4o-mini, claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022 → claude-sonnet-4-6). New versions may improve some tasks while regressing others. Before switching to a new model in production: run your full regression suite on the new model; compare all metrics to the current model baseline; identify regressions on specific query types; decide whether the aggregate quality improvement justifies the regression in specific areas. This prevents 'model upgrade surprises' where a new model is better on benchmarks but worse on your specific use case.

whenWhy.title: RAG pipeline component testing
whenWhy.body: Changes to any RAG component (chunking strategy, embedding model, retrieval k, re-ranker) can affect end-to-end quality. Regression tests catch these: baseline metrics from before the change vs. metrics after. Component-level regression tests are more diagnostic: test the retrieval stage independently (hit@k before and after changing the embedding model) before testing end-to-end quality. This localises regressions to the component that changed.

deepDiveIntro: LLM regression testing is probabilistic, not deterministic. Traditional software tests assert exact outputs (assertEqual(result, 'expected')). LLM tests assert statistical properties (assertGreater(mean_score, threshold), assertAlmostEqual(new_score, baseline_score, delta=0.05)). This requires: a fixed evaluation dataset, a defined scoring function (LLM-as-judge, task success function, embedding similarity), baseline scores recorded from the current production system, and a statistical significance test to distinguish real regressions from noise.

The evaluation dataset is the critical asset. A good regression test dataset covers: representative queries (reflects actual production traffic distribution), edge cases (queries the system has historically struggled with), adversarial inputs (designed to probe weak points), and key capability queries (tests for must-pass behaviors). The dataset should be versioned alongside the code; changes to the dataset require re-baselining.

deepDive.heading: Regression Test Architecture in CI/CD
deepDive.body: CI/CD integration pattern: (1) Trigger: on every PR that changes a prompt file, model config, or RAG component; (2) Evaluation run: spin up the application with the PR's changes; run the golden dataset through the evaluation pipeline (typically 50-200 examples for CI speed); score with automated metrics; (3) Comparison: compare current scores to baseline (stored in a JSON file committed to the repo, or fetched from an experiment tracking service like MLflow/Braintrust); (4) Pass/fail threshold: fail the CI check if any metric drops > X% from baseline (typical: 5% for key metrics, 10% for secondary metrics); (5) Report: post a summary comment on the PR with before/after metrics. Tools: DeepEval + pytest for inline quality assertions; Braintrust for full experiment tracking and comparison. [1]

deepDive.heading: Statistical Significance in Regression Detection
deepDive.body: LLM evaluation scores are noisy (the same input may score differently on different runs due to model non-determinism). A score drop of 0.02 may be noise; a drop of 0.10 may be a real regression. Statistical testing: (1) Run both baseline and candidate on the same N examples; (2) Compute a paired difference test (Wilcoxon signed-rank test for non-normal distributions) to assess whether the score difference is statistically significant (p < 0.05); (3) Calculate the minimum detectable effect size for your N — larger N detects smaller regressions. Rule of thumb: N=100 detects a 5-point mean score drop (effect size 0.5 SD) at 80% power. For high-stakes changes, use N=200-500. For routine PR checks, N=50-100 is sufficient with a generous detection threshold (10% drop). [2]

deepDive.heading: Failure Mode Taxonomy for Regression Analysis
deepDive.body: When a regression is detected, classify it: (1) Faithfulness regression — the model is generating more claims not supported by retrieved context; cause: new model is more 'creative', or new prompt is less constraining; (2) Retrieval regression — lower hit@k means a chunking or embedding change broke retrieval; (3) Format regression — outputs violate expected format constraints (missing required JSON fields, wrong output length); (4) Safety regression — content filter triggers more often, or harmful content appears in outputs not previously flagged; (5) Latency regression — p95 latency increased by > 20%; (6) Cost regression — average cost per query increased. Different regression types require different fixes. Always check which examples regressed, not just the mean — a small mean drop may hide a catastrophic failure on a specific query type. [1]

takeaways: LLM regression tests: compare quality metrics (not exact outputs) before/after changes on a fixed golden dataset. [1]
CI/CD integration: run on every PR affecting prompt/model/RAG config; fail if metric drops > 5-10% from baseline. [1]
Statistical significance: paired test on N=100+ examples to distinguish noise from real regression. [2]
Classify regressions by type (faithfulness, retrieval, format, safety, latency, cost) to localise the cause. [1]
Dataset: representative + edge cases + adversarial + must-pass — version alongside code, re-baseline on dataset changes. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you maintain the regression test dataset as the product evolves?
edgeCases.a: Dataset drift: the product's query distribution changes over time, making the old dataset unrepresentative. Dataset maintenance practices: (1) Monthly review — sample 50-100 recent production queries; identify new query types not covered by the existing dataset; add 5-10 new examples per new query type; (2) Failure injection — when production incidents occur (user complaint, quality alert), add the failing example to the regression dataset so it's always tested going forward; (3) Dataset versioning — tag the dataset with a version; when the dataset changes significantly (> 20% new examples), re-run the baseline to update reference scores; track which dataset version a baseline was computed on; (4) Category balance — ensure the dataset covers all major feature areas (don't have 80% customer-service queries and 5% technical queries if production is 50/50). A stale dataset gives false confidence: tests pass on old patterns but miss regressions on new ones.

edgeCases.q: What should you do when a regression is detected but the overall metrics don't drop (a mixed-result change)?
edgeCases.a: A 'mixed regression' occurs when a change improves some examples and regresses others — the mean score stays flat. This is common when changing to a new model: better at some tasks, worse at others. Analysis steps: (1) Segment analysis — compute metrics separately for each query category (retrieval questions, summarisation, Q&A, code); identify which categories regressed; (2) Example-level diff — show the specific examples where the score dropped by > 0.2 points; read them to understand the regression pattern; (3) Impact assessment — estimate what fraction of production traffic falls into the regressed category (a 10% drop on a category that accounts for 2% of traffic may be acceptable); (4) Decision: if the regression is on a high-traffic or high-stakes category, don't deploy the change; add targeted examples to the failing category to the dataset and retest after a targeted fix. Document the decision and trade-offs.
